picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: AVGO headlines the June 3 earnings report guiding $22B revenue (+47% YoY) with AI semiconductor revenue expected at $10.7B (+140% YoY) — the strongest near-term fundamental catalyst in the entire universe. EPS consensus has been revised up 11.1% over the past 90 days to $2.40, Oppenheimer projects a 30% QoQ surge in AI sales, and UBS raised its price target to $490. The custom silicon + networking moat (XPUs for Google, Meta, Apple) is a multi-year structural advantage that is not replicated by any single competitor, and management has projected AI chip revenues could exceed $100B in 2027, implying the current setup is still early cycle within a secular trend.
picks.catalyst: Fiscal Q2 2026 earnings + Q3 guidance on June 3 after market close — AI chip revenue trajectory and hyperscaler XPU design-win commentary are the primary variables
picks.catalystWindow: Next 72 hours (June 3 earnings), then 2–3 quarters for $100B AI revenue pathway validation
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$447, near all-time high of $448.90, up 29% YTD; options market implies ±10.65% post-earnings move. P/E ~85x trailing but ~30–32x forward on FY2027 consensus — premium justified by 47%+ revenue growth and 77% gross margins. Enter pre-earnings with the understanding that a beat-and-raise is the base case given the 11.1% upward EPS revision trend.
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margin compression below 74% for two consecutive quarters, any loss of a hyperscaler XPU design win (Google, Meta, Apple), or AI semiconductor revenue guide meaningfully below $12B for Q3
picks.keyRisk: A 'sell the news' event post-June 3 if AI revenue merely meets rather than significantly beats the $10.7B guide, combined with insider selling of $356M over the past 3 months suggesting some distribution near highs

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: GOOGL is the highest-composite-score name in the momentum screen — Search grew 19% to $60.4B, YouTube ads +11%, and Google Cloud surged 63% to $20B with backlog nearly doubling to $460B in Q1 2026. Berkshire nearly tripled its stake (+204%, ~57.8M shares, ~$16.6B) making it the 5th largest holding, while DA Davidson projects Alphabet's TPUs could capture 20% of the AI infrastructure market and become a $900B business — providing both a cloud hyperscaler and chip alternative moat that directly hedges NVDA concentration risk. Analysts still consider the stock undervalued despite a 120% 12-month run.
picks.catalyst: DA Davidson TPU monetization announcement + continued Google Cloud acceleration toward Q2 2026 earnings (late July); May CPI print on June 11 as macro read-through for ad market
picks.catalystWindow: June–August 2026 (Q2 earnings, ongoing TPU customer expansion announcements)
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately $376 intraday June 1, down ~2.5% on the session as broader Mag-7 digests gains; stock has more than doubled over the past 12 months yet analysts call it undervalued on forward multiples relative to cloud growth trajectory. Constructive entry on any weakness below $380.
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud revenue growth decelerates below 40% YoY for two consecutive quarters, DOJ antitrust ruling forces structural remedy on Search, or AI Overviews cannibalizes Search ad yields materially
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust remedies (Chrome/Search divestiture scenario) represent a binary legal risk with a potential ruling in late 2026 that could reprice the stock 15–20%

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA surged 4.48% on June 1 following Jensen Huang's COMPUTEX keynote unveiling new RTX Spark laptop processors, and Goldman Sachs reaffirmed a Buy rating post-Computex. Record Q1 fiscal sales of $81.6B with data center revenue up 92% YoY confirm the Blackwell architecture is ramping at full velocity, and DA Davidson added NVDA to its Best of Breed Bison list with a $300 price target noting the margin advantage remains 'difficult to challenge.' BofA reiterated Buy with a $320 target in June 2026. The AI capex supercycle — now revised to $800B in 2026 and $1.16T in 2027 — keeps NVDA at the structural center of the largest infrastructure buildout in history.
picks.catalyst: COMPUTEX 2026 product launches (June 2–5 in Taipei) and next earnings release estimated August 25 — any Blackwell next-generation architecture preview or new hyperscaler partnership announcement
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$212 intraday June 1 (~$5.1T market cap); 40.6x forward earnings is reasonable given 50%+ EPS growth trajectory. Export restriction risk to China is partially priced in after prior selloffs. DA Davidson $300 PT implies ~42% upside. RSI elevated post-Computex surge — consider scaling into any pullback below $200.
picks.exitTrigger: Any Blackwell demand normalization signal in guidance, meaningful export restriction expansion that includes Hopper-tier chips for allied nations, or two consecutive quarters of data center revenue growth below 60% YoY
picks.keyRisk: U.S. export restriction escalation targeting Chinese entities worldwide could carve out 15–20% of total addressable revenue in one regulatory action

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: AMZN is the rare mega-cap with convergent smart-money conviction — Tepper nearly doubled his stake (+98%, now 15.2% of Appaloosa's portfolio as #1 holding), Ackman added ~1.84M shares moving it to his #2 position, and ARK also added. AWS margin trajectory as AI workloads monetize is the core thesis: AI capex investment is now converting into accelerating cloud revenue at higher margins, and Amazon's logistics network provides an inflation-resistant moat that competitors cannot replicate at scale. Druckenmiller doubled his AMZN calls position, adding options leverage to the same thesis.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July/early August) with focus on AWS operating margin expansion and AI revenue contribution — any Project Kuiper satellite internet update is an additional optionality unlock
picks.catalystWindow: July–August 2026 (Q2 earnings); ongoing AWS re:Invent-style announcements through Q3
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$270 intraday June 1, down ~1.3% on the session. Stock is at roughly 35–38x forward earnings on FY2027 consensus — below NVDA and AVGO despite comparable or superior FCF growth trajectory. Three of the world's sharpest macro minds (Tepper, Ackman, Druckenmiller) are simultaneously adding — a rare convergence signal.
picks.exitTrigger: AWS revenue growth decelerates below 25% for two consecutive quarters, or AI capex guide-up without corresponding margin expansion signals a deteriorating ROI narrative
picks.keyRisk: Elevated AI capex could compress FCF if AWS pricing power weakens against Azure and Google Cloud — a ROI recession scenario hits AMZN's multiple hardest

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: GS is the highest-quality expression of the Financials sector rotation thesis — capital markets, M&A advisory, and trading desk revenues all benefit from the steepening 2s10s curve (+47bps), improving deal activity, and a potential rate-cut resumption. Financials Q1 2026 revenue grew +10.9% YoY, the sector trades at a discount to the S&P 500 on forward P/E, and GS specifically has the highest operating leverage to a reactivation of the IPO and M&A pipelines that have been dormant since 2022. It has remained a core high-conviction position for seven consecutive sessions in the prior positioning record.
picks.catalyst: June 16–17 FOMC meeting — any signal of renewed rate-cut trajectory reopens the M&A/IPO pipeline and directly benefits GS advisory and equity underwriting revenues
picks.entryNote: Trading at a discount to the S&P 500 on forward P/E (~12–13x) vs. the index at 20–21x; GS has the highest ROE among bulge-bracket banks. The 2s10s at +47bps provides positive NIM tailwind. Technically constructive — held above 200-day MA through April volatility.
picks.exitTrigger: Credit spreads widen materially (HY above 500bps), a recession signal that freezes M&A activity for 2+ quarters, or a trading revenue shock from a client default/blow-up
picks.keyRisk: A fiscal crisis / bond vigilante event (Tail Risk #1) would widen credit spreads and freeze deal flow simultaneously — the worst-case scenario for GS's revenue mix

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is the defense/aerospace anchor in the portfolio — sitting at the intersection of the rearmament supercycle (NATO defense spending mandates rising toward 3% of GDP), the Iran conflict geopolitical premium, and commercial aerospace recovery. Industrials blended earnings growth surged to +20.9% in Q1 2026, and RTX specifically benefits from both the aftermarket engine services business (Pratt & Whitney GTF engines powering a replacement cycle) and Raytheon missile/defense systems with multi-year government contracts. This is a low-correlation name to the AI technology cluster that provides genuine sector diversification with visible, contracted backlog.
picks.catalyst: NATO summit defense spending commitments (June 2026) and any Iran conflict escalation that accelerates U.S. Patriot/missile defense procurement — next earnings Q3 2026
picks.catalystWindow: June–September 2026 (NATO summit, Iran situation, Q2 earnings in July)
picks.entryNote: Trading at roughly 20–22x forward earnings — a premium to defense peers but justified by the commercial aerospace kicker (Pratt engine services backlog multi-year). XLI delivered +25.66% total return over the past year; RTX has been a consistent performer through the geopolitical tape. Seven consecutive sessions as a top-6 position in prior book.
picks.exitTrigger: GTF engine crack resolution costs exceed $10B total, Iran conflict resolves diplomatically and defense budgets are cut, or Pratt loses a major international engine competition
picks.keyRisk: GTF engine inspection liabilities remain an overhang — any new material liability estimate increase could reset 2026 EPS guidance by 10–15%

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: XOM is the highest-quality expression of the energy sector's binary geopolitical positioning — the cheapest sector in the S&P 500 trading at ~32% discount to its own 10-year average on both absolute and relative valuation metrics, with Morgan Stanley's base case assuming crude returns to ~$90/barrel by year-end. Exxon's Pioneer acquisition integration is accelerating Permian production cost advantages, and the $20B+ annual buyback provides a floor on the stock. Energy EPS estimates have just begun receiving a boost from elevated Iran-conflict oil prices, and the name provides explicit portfolio insurance against the Strait of Hormuz escalation tail risk (Tail Risk #3).
picks.catalyst: May CPI print (June 11) as oil-price pass-through measure; Iran diplomatic outcome (binary); Q2 earnings (late July) with Pioneer synergy update
picks.catalystWindow: June–August 2026 (CPI, Iran resolution or escalation, earnings)
picks.entryNote: XLE closed at $56.29 on May 30 — energy is the only sector trading at a discount to the S&P 500 AND to its own 10-year average. At ~12–13x forward earnings with a 3.5%+ dividend yield, the margin of safety is the best in the large-cap universe. Midstream would be more stable, but XOM's buyback and Permian optionality justify upstream exposure.
picks.exitTrigger: Iran/Hormuz diplomatic resolution driving crude back below $65/barrel sustained for 60+ days, or pioneered integration costs exceeding revised synergy targets by more than 20%
picks.keyRisk: A genuine Iran de-escalation (the largest positive macro surprise for 2026 per the macro briefing) would drop oil 10–15% and compress XOM's earnings by 15–20% — the position is a hedge that could become a drag

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is the highest-quality financial infrastructure compounder in the universe — a near-duopoly in credit ratings (with Moody's), combined with market intelligence (Platts, Market Intelligence) that is deeply embedded in every institutional workflow. In a late-cycle environment where credit issuance activity reaccelerates as rates stabilize, SPGI's ratings revenue has extraordinary operating leverage — roughly 85% incremental margins on new rating mandates. The Moody's downgrade of U.S. sovereign debt in May 2025 paradoxically highlights SPGI's ratings business pricing power, as more entities require ratings in a higher-spread environment. ROIC ~25%+, FCF conversion ~100%, 50+ year dividend growth streak.
picks.catalyst: FOMC June 16–17 any signal of resumed cutting cycle reopens high-yield and investment-grade issuance pipelines; Q2 2026 earnings in late July will show ratings revenue momentum
picks.catalystWindow: June 16–17 FOMC decision; July–August Q2 earnings
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 27–30x forward earnings — premium to the market but historically justified given near-monopoly pricing power and 85%+ operating margins in ratings. At eight consecutive sessions as a core position, thesis remains fully intact: a steepening curve and improving deal activity are the direct revenue drivers.
picks.exitTrigger: Antitrust action threatening NRSRO duopoly status, or sustained credit market freeze for 3+ quarters that collapses new issuance below 2022 trough levels
picks.keyRisk: A full credit market seizure (HY spreads >600bps) would freeze new issuance and collapse ratings revenue — SPGI is deeply procyclical despite its perceived defensive moat

picks.name: Merck & Co. Inc.
picks.rationale: MRK is the highest-conviction Healthcare value expression — trading near multi-year lows after Keytruda biosimilar overhang concerns have created a 'fallen quality' setup. The healthcare sector has underperformed the S&P 500 by ~60 percentage points over the last 2.5 years and biotech is trading at a 15% discount to broader markets on forward P/E. MRK specifically has the Keytruda franchise (still 5+ years of exclusivity in most indications), a deep oncology pipeline (TIGIT, subcutaneous Keytruda formulation), and the Daiichi Sankyo ADC licensing deal providing a next-generation oncology platform. The FDA has proven support for a strong biopharma industry with new approval acceleration programs, reducing tail regulatory risk.
picks.catalyst: Subcutaneous Keytruda FDA approval decision (expected H2 2026) which extends effective exclusivity; pipeline readouts for next-generation oncology combinations; IRA drug pricing impact becoming better understood
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 12–14x forward earnings — among the cheapest large-cap pharma in the U.S. relative to pipeline quality. Dividend yield ~3.5% provides carry while waiting for the pipeline inflection. The sector's lowest relative P/E ratios in history create asymmetric upside if Healthcare re-rates even modestly.
picks.exitTrigger: Keytruda combination trial failures across multiple tumor types signaling resistance mechanisms, IRA price negotiation terms set below market expectations on Keytruda, or two consecutive quarters of top-line guidance cuts
picks.keyRisk: IRA drug pricing negotiation for Keytruda — if CMS sets a reference price materially below current net pricing, 2028–2030 EPS estimates could require a 15–20% reset

picks.name: Vistra Corp.
picks.rationale: VST is the portfolio's AI power demand play — the purest-play independent power producer with nuclear and natural gas generation assets positioned to sign long-term power purchase agreements with hyperscale data centers. Utilities reported the highest revenue growth surprise of any sector in Q1 2026 (+14.3% vs. +9.4% estimate), and David Tepper doubled his Vistra Energy stake in Q1 2026. The stock sits at the intersection of the AI data center power demand supercycle and the geopolitical energy premium from Iran, giving it dual-catalyst tailwinds that differentiate it from rate-sensitive traditional utilities (which are explicitly avoided). CEG and NRG are alternatives, but VST's merchant generation exposure gives it the highest EPS torque to power price spikes.
picks.catalyst: New hyperscaler PPA (power purchase agreement) announcement — any data center power deal with a major cloud provider would confirm VST's nuclear capacity premium; Q2 2026 earnings in August
picks.entryNote: Utilities sector has started to underperform after a strong run per sector rotation data — VST is a selective hold within a consolidating sector. Position sized at medium conviction (5%) to reflect the sector's post-run technical status while maintaining exposure to the AI power theme. Tepper's doubling of the position provides smart-money validation.
picks.exitTrigger: Power price normalization below $50/MWh in PJM sustained for two quarters, loss of a key nuclear license renewal, or hyperscaler capex cuts reducing power demand forecasts materially
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory risk on merchant power pricing — FERC capacity market rule changes could suppress PJM power prices and directly compress VST's merchant generation margins

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: GLD is the portfolio's primary macro hedge — providing explicit protection against Tail Risk #1 (U.S. fiscal crisis / bond vigilante shock) and Tail Risk #3 (Iran escalation / energy price shock). No major agency holds the U.S. at AAA, the 30-year yield is above 4.94%, and the 'One Big Beautiful Bill' adds an estimated $3.4T to the deficit through 2034. GLD has pulled back ~20% from its 2026 highs, offering a re-entry point with real option value. Goldman Sachs targets $5,000 gold and UBS revised its year-end 2026 target to $5,500/oz — implying significant upside from current levels around $408–$414 (intraday June 1).
picks.catalyst: May CPI print (June 11) — a hot print above 0.4% MoM core would accelerate gold's fiscal hedge bid; any Iran escalation event or failed Treasury auction
picks.catalystWindow: June 11 CPI; ongoing through the Iran geopolitical situation; August debt ceiling X-date
picks.entryNote: GLD intraday range June 1 is $408.24–$414.09, roughly 20% below its 2026 highs — the pullback creates a favorable risk/reward entry. At current levels the gold-to-real-yield relationship implies room for re-expansion if the 10-year real yield rolls over on any growth scare.
picks.exitTrigger: Iran diplomatic resolution driving a sustained oil price decline + Fed successful disinflation bringing core PCE sustainably below 2.2% + fiscal consolidation legislation passing Congress — a triple-catalyst relief that removes all three gold drivers simultaneously
picks.keyRisk: A genuine soft landing with falling inflation and a balanced budget would collapse the gold thesis — low probability but the scenario that most directly challenges GLD's defensive rationale

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD is the portfolio's quality-income anchor — ~3.5% dividend yield, 0.06% expense ratio, and exposure to ~100 dividend-paying U.S. equities screened for cash flow, dividend growth, and financial strength. It provides low-correlation behavior relative to the AI-heavy growth book, with sector tilts toward Financials, Industrials, Consumer Staples, and Healthcare that complement rather than duplicate the individual stock positions. In the April 2026 correction, dividend-quality factors materially outperformed high-multiple tech. The 3.5% yield competes favorably with short-duration bonds while providing equity upside in a soft landing.
picks.catalyst: Any FOMC signal of renewed rate-cut trajectory accelerates rotation into dividend-quality equities as investors price in lower reinvestment rates for T-bills; SCHD benefits from yield compression
picks.catalystWindow: June 16–17 FOMC and onward through year-end 2026
picks.entryNote: SCHD has seen persistent institutional outflows (3-month net flows -$1.75B) which has kept it at attractive relative valuations vs. growth; contra-trend positioning here is deliberate. The dividend yield at 3.5% is additive to total return in a slow-growth late-cycle environment.
picks.exitTrigger: Dividend cuts accelerate across the fund's top 20 holdings (signaling a genuine recession), or a sharp rotation into growth causes the fund to meaningfully underperform T-bills for 4+ consecutive quarters
picks.keyRisk: In a deep recession, dividend-payers cut dividends — SCHD would not be immune to a 2008-style 35–40% drawdown if credit markets seize

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: BRK.B is the portfolio's fortress balance sheet anchor — $397.4B in cash and equivalents generating >$15B annually in T-bill income at current rates, providing an automatic buffer that no other equity offers. Greg Abel's first full quarter demonstrated decisive capital allocation (nearly tripling the Alphabet stake, exiting 15+ positions) while maintaining the $397B cash hoard — an implicit macro warning signal that serves the portfolio as a built-in optionality reserve. At ~1.5x book value with near-zero earnings multiple sensitivity, BRK.B screens as the closest equivalent to a free lunch in a market trading at Shiller P/E >42, and it has strong negative correlation to panic events.
picks.catalyst: Any market dislocation creates a deployment opportunity for Berkshire's $397B cash — Abel's demonstrated willingness to act decisively (Alphabet stake tripling) signals the capital will be deployed at the right price; next earnings release in August
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing optionality; August 2026 Q2 earnings for cash deployment update
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~1.5x book value — historically a reasonable entry point. The T-bill income alone (~$15B+ annually) makes BRK.B one of the highest-quality income generators in the defensive sleeve without the interest rate duration risk of long-duration bonds.
picks.exitTrigger: Greg Abel demonstrates significantly different capital allocation philosophy than Buffett's — e.g., large leveraged acquisition or aggressive share buyback at excessive premium to book — or insurance underwriting losses create a persistent drag on book value growth
picks.keyRisk: Key-person transition risk from Buffett to Abel is unquantifiable — market perception of Berkshire's management quality is partially a function of Buffett's reputation that Abel has not yet fully earned

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF provides intermediate-duration Treasury exposure (7–10 year) at a current yield of approximately 4.45% (tracking the 10-year note), offering a 4.45% carry that competes directly with equity earnings yield (~3.11% trailing on the S&P 500) — the rare moment when high-quality duration pays more than owning the index. Sized at medium conviction given the asymmetry: if the May CPI is hot (>0.4% MoM) IEF loses 1–2%, but if the FOMC pivots dovish or a growth scare materializes, IEF gains 4–8% in a risk-off scenario. Positioned between TLT (too long duration in a potential bond vigilante event) and T-bills (no price appreciation optionality).
picks.catalyst: June 11 May CPI print and June 16–17 FOMC meeting — any dovish surprise (lower inflation, softer growth) drives 10-year yields lower and IEF higher by 3–5%; a growth scare is the ultimate catalyst
picks.catalystWindow: June 11 CPI, June 16–17 FOMC — both are binary events for duration
picks.entryNote: 2-year note at 3.98%, 10-year at 4.45% — the 2s10s at +47bps is modestly positive, providing some carry advantage to the intermediate part of the curve. IEF at current yields offers the best risk-adjusted carry in the fixed income universe without the 30-year's fiscal risk (30-year at 4.94%, 97th percentile on 5-year lookback).
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year Treasury yield breaks above 5.0% on a sustained basis (hot CPI + hawkish FOMC), or U.S. fiscal situation deteriorates to the point where bond vigilantes force a re-rating of U.S. sovereign credit risk that pushes the curve higher across all tenors
picks.keyRisk: A hot May CPI print (>0.4% MoM core) combined with a hawkish FOMC surprise (hike bias) would push the 10-year through 4.70–4.80% and create a 3–4% mark-to-market loss on IEF in a single week

picks.name: Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO is the portfolio's low-beta consumer staples anchor — 60+ consecutive years of dividend growth, ~$45B in annual revenue, and pricing power that has survived multiple inflationary cycles, making it relevant in the current stagflationary regime. Beta of ~0.55 means KO characteristically outperforms the S&P 500 by 15–25% relative in sharp drawdown events (2022, April 2026 Liberation Day). The ~3.2% dividend yield provides carry that partially offsets the opportunity cost vs. T-bills, and the relative valuation at ~21–22x forward earnings is a genuine discount to the S&P 500's 20–21x when quality-adjusted. Berkshire Hathaway (one of the world's most sophisticated capital allocators) maintains KO as a core long-term position.
picks.catalyst: Risk-off macro event (hot CPI, hawkish FOMC, Iran escalation) drives rotation into consumer staples; next quarterly earnings in July with volume data confirming pricing power maintained
picks.catalystWindow: Any macro shock event in June–August 2026 would trigger the defensive rotation; July earnings as fundamental confirmation
picks.entryNote: KO has remained in the defensive sleeve for all seven prior sessions — thesis fully intact. At current levels (~$73–75 estimated), the stock is not cheap on an absolute basis but offers the most reliable risk-off behavior in the defensive sleeve. The 3.2% yield and 60-year dividend growth streak are irreplaceable in a concentrated book.
picks.exitTrigger: Volume declines exceed 5% for two consecutive quarters (signaling brand deterioration or consumer trade-down failure), dividend growth streak ends, or a sustained inflation normalization drives investors out of defensives into higher-yielding cyclicals en masse
picks.keyRisk: Obesity drug penetration (GLP-1 medications) reducing sugar/beverage consumption is an emerging structural demand risk that is difficult to quantify but directionally negative over the 3–5 year horizon

summary: The portfolio is constructed for a late-cycle stagflationary regime with a Cautiously Bullish bias — maintaining full equity exposure through high-quality AI infrastructure compounders (AVGO, GOOGL, NVDA, AMZN) while simultaneously hedging the three dominant tail risks: U.S. fiscal deterioration (GLD, IEF, BRK.B), Iran energy shock (XOM as explicit hedge), and AI capex cycle reversal (sector diversification into Financials/GS/SPGI, Industrials/RTX, Healthcare/MRK, and Utilities/VST). The growth book carries over eight of the last ten positions with only minor rank adjustments — AVGO moves to #1 given the imminent June 3 earnings catalyst with a +47% YoY revenue guide and 10.65% implied options move; LLY is rotated out in favor of MRK on relative valuation grounds within Healthcare, as MRK trades at 12–14x forward earnings vs. LLY's premium multiple, providing better asymmetry in a sector that has underperformed by 60 percentage points over 2.5 years. The dominant risk being underwritten is a hot May CPI print on June 11 combined with a hawkish FOMC surprise on June 16–17 that simultaneously compresses equity multiples, pushes the 10-year above 4.70%, and forces a reassessment of the AI capex ROI thesis — the defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) is explicitly sized to provide 25–30% portfolio buffer against this scenario.
diversificationNote: The growth book spans five distinct GICS sectors: Information Technology (AVGO, NVDA — custom silicon/data center infrastructure and compute acceleration, distinct value chain positions), Communication Services (GOOGL — search/cloud/TPU), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN — cloud/logistics), Financials (GS, SPGI — capital markets and ratings duopoly, both procyclical but with different revenue drivers), Industrials (RTX — defense/aerospace), Energy (XOM — upstream/Permian), Health Care (MRK — large-cap pharma/oncology), and Utilities (VST — merchant power/nuclear AI demand); the two IT names share secular AI infrastructure exposure but occupy complementary positions in the value chain (AVGO: custom silicon/networking vs. NVDA: GPU compute); the defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures — gold commodity hedge (GLD), broad dividend/quality income (SCHD), diversified holding company with fortress balance sheet (BRK.B), intermediate Treasury duration (IEF), and consumer staples low-beta equity (KO) — with no duplicate exposure types.

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: AVGO is the single highest-conviction idea in the book heading into June 3 Q2 earnings, with company guidance calling for $22B revenue (+47% YoY) and $10.7B AI semiconductor revenue (+140% YoY) — both well above the analyst consensus of ~$20.4-20.5B. Broadcom's custom silicon moat (Google TPUs, Meta AI chips, Microsoft processors) creates deep switching costs and ~77% gross margins, while CEO Hock Tan has stated the company has 'line of sight' to $100B+ in AI chip revenue by 2027. Druckenmiller initiated a new position in Q1 2026, and 35 of 42 covering analysts rate it Strong Buy with a ~$470 average price target.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2026 earnings report on June 3, 2026 — guided for $22B revenue (+47% YoY) and $10.7B AI revenue (+140% YoY), with Oppenheimer predicting 30% QoQ AI sales growth
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$421 vs. 52-week range of well below current; base-building consolidation over the past month is a textbook calm-before-the-storm setup. Full-year EPS consensus of $9.95 (+76.7% YoY) and FY2027 EPS of $16.32 (+64%) justify the premium vs. peers.
picks.exitTrigger: AI semiconductor revenue guidance for Q3 misses $10.7B or decelerates below 80% YoY growth; hyperscaler customer concentration risk materializes via a Google or Meta custom silicon pullback
picks.keyRisk: Customer concentration — Google, Meta, and Microsoft together represent a dominant share of AI revenue; any hyperscaler capex pause or pivot to in-house silicon would immediately reprice AVGO

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Alphabet remains the cleanest setup in the mega-cap AI space: Google Cloud revenue grew +63% in Q1 2026, nearly 800% growth for GenAI-built products, and 350M paid subscriptions for Gemini. The stock is considered undervalued by Wall Street analysts despite a 20%+ YTD gain, and Berkshire Hathaway — under new CEO Greg Abel — tripled its stake to $16.6B in Q1 2026, making GOOGL its 5th-largest holding. No extension flag; momentum is driven by fundamental acceleration rather than multiple expansion.
picks.catalyst: June FOMC (June 17-18) risk-on relief if Warsh holds rates steady; ongoing Google I/O product cycle with Gemini Ultra 2 enterprise monetization updates in Q2
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 20x forward earnings — a meaningful discount to its Communication Services peers and well below NVDA/META multiples given its revenue scale. Berkshire's triple-down signals deep value conviction at current prices.
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 40% YoY or DOJ antitrust remedy forces search revenue sharing that materially impairs Google's ~90% search monetization model
picks.keyRisk: DOJ search antitrust structural remedy — a forced default-browser divestiture or revenue sharing with competitors is the single largest unquantified risk in the thesis

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA delivered record Q1 FY2027 revenue of $81.6B (+85% YoY) with data center revenue of $75B (+92% YoY), $49B FCF, and 74.9% gross margin — 8 consecutive EPS beats. Hyperscaler capex is guided to reach $485-515B in 2026, with multi-year supply commitments stretching into 2027 providing extraordinary revenue visibility. Despite the 'buy the rumor, sell the news' post-earnings reaction, the fundamental bull case is entirely intact and the stock has consolidated into a healthier technical base.
picks.catalyst: GTC/developer conference updates and Q2 FY2027 earnings in August — Blackwell Ultra and Rubin architecture roadmap announcements could re-accelerate the multiple
picks.entryNote: Trading near 30x forward earnings — an extension flag from the momentum screen, but justified by 85% revenue growth. Post-May 20 earnings consolidation offers a better entry than the pre-print highs. Key technical support at the 50-day moving average.
picks.exitTrigger: H20 China ban materially expands to limit Blackwell exports further; gross margin falls below 70% (from 74.9%) due to custom silicon competition from AVGO/MRVL taking hyperscaler share
picks.keyRisk: Export control escalation targeting Blackwell chips to China/Middle East markets, combined with a hyperscaler capex pause that cuts forward revenue guidance by 10%+

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Amazon's AWS cloud revenue growth reaccelerated to +28% in Q1 2026 (vs. +24% and +20% in prior quarters), Trainium revenue commitments exceed $225B, and the market has validated that AI capex is converting to AWS margin expansion. picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman is the premier capital markets and M&A franchise, the primary beneficiary of the M&A revival that is driving Financials earnings revisions upward — the only non-tech/energy sector with positive EPS revision trajectory. picks.exitTrigger: M&A pipeline freezes due to rate-hike shock or regulatory blocking of major deals; credit spreads blow out above 500bps HY OAS creating mark-to-market losses on the leveraged finance book

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is the highest-quality defense and aerospace compounder with dual exposure to commercial aerospace recovery (Pratt & Whitney engine deliveries ramping) and accelerating global defense spending driven by the Iran conflict and NATO budget escalations. Defense budgets are in a structural multi-year upcycle, and RTX's backlog provides 4-5 years of revenue visibility. The Iran conflict is directly driving procurement urgency for Patriot missile systems, Raytheon's Tomahawk, and Pratt's F135 engines — making RTX a direct geopolitical hedge within the equity book.

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: Exxon is the only S&P 500 sector trading at a ~32% discount to both the broad market AND its own 10-year average — providing the deepest valuation margin of safety in the universe. Oil prices are elevated 50%+ since the Iran conflict began, generating extraordinary FCF for disciplined operators like Exxon, which has the lowest breakeven cost structure among majors. Berkshire trimmed Chevron but energy remains a core portfolio hedge, and XOM's Guyana production ramp provides volume growth independent of price. The stagflation macro regime (our base case) historically rewards commodity producers above all.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — elevated oil prices from Iran conflict should drive FCF materially above $15B quarterly; any ceasefire would reduce near-term price risk but validate the structural thesis
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~32% discount to its own 10-year valuation average — the deepest sector-level undervaluation in the S&P 500 universe. picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI reported strong Q1 2026 earnings beating estimates with adjusted EPS of $4.97 and revenue of $4.2B, yet the stock is trading ~28% below its 52-week high of $579. The newly board-approved Mobility spin-off into Mobility Global Inc. is a classic value-unlock catalyst — separating a lower-multiple automotive data business from the core high-margin Ratings, Indices, and Market Intelligence franchises that carry 40%+ EBITDA margins. Goldman Sachs reaffirmed Buy yesterday, and the 12-month consensus price target implies +26% upside from current levels near $416.
picks.catalyst: Mobility Global Inc. spin-off execution and timeline clarity — the Board approved the separation on May 21, 2026; investor day and spin terms will reset the sum-of-the-parts valuation significantly
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~26x P/E vs. a historical average near 34-38x for the core data/ratings franchise — the discount reflects spin-off uncertainty and macro credit market concerns. At $416 vs. $579 high, downside risk is asymmetrically limited. SPGI targets 7.5-10% organic growth and commits to returning 75%+ of FCF to shareholders.
picks.exitTrigger: Ratings revenue collapses due to credit market freeze (HY spreads widening to 600bps+) cutting into issuance volumes; Mobility spin-off timeline delays beyond Q2 2027 remove the value-unlock catalyst
picks.keyRisk: A Fed rate hike shock freezes the high-yield and investment-grade issuance market, directly impressing SPGI's Ratings segment revenue which is highly correlated to debt capital market volumes

picks.name: Vistra Energy Corp.
picks.rationale: VST is the smart money's backdoor AI trade — Tepper doubled his position in Q1 2026, betting that power grid capacity is the binding constraint on AI data center expansion. VST operates the largest competitive power generation fleet in the US, with significant natural gas and nuclear exposure that directly benefits from surging data center load growth. This is a non-tech, non-semi exposure to the AI capex supercycle with utility-like contractual revenues but growth stock upside from electricity demand re-rating.
picks.catalyst: Data center power purchase agreement (PPA) announcements — any major hyperscaler multi-year PPA with VST for dedicated nuclear or gas generation would immediately re-rate the stock
picks.entryNote: The stock has pulled back from highs and is building a base. Unlike traditional utilities (NEE, DUK), VST is a competitive merchant generator with pricing power in deregulated markets — it does not suffer the same duration sensitivity as regulated utilities at 5.18% 30yr rates. Trading at approximately 18-20x forward earnings.
picks.exitTrigger: Natural gas prices collapse below $2.50/mmBtu sustained, compressing merchant power margins; federal energy regulation reversal capping merchant power prices in ERCOT/PJM markets
picks.keyRisk: ERCOT (Texas) regulatory risk — political intervention in power pricing during extreme heat events could cap VST's earnings power precisely when demand is highest

picks.name: Merck & Co. Inc.
picks.rationale: Merck is a deep-value, counter-cyclical compounder with a pipeline anchored by Keytruda (oncology), WINREVAIR (pulmonary arterial hypertension), and the MK-0616 cardiovascular franchise — offsetting the Keytruda patent cliff with next-generation assets. Healthcare is the most beaten-down sector YTD (-7% vs. SPY +5%) and trades at a significant discount to the broad market, creating asymmetric upside for patient capital. Managed care (UNH) and pharma drag are separable sub-sector stories; Merck's pure pharmaceutical exposure avoids the DOJ/insurance regulatory risk.
picks.catalyst: Phase 3 data readouts for MK-0616 (oral PCSK9 inhibitor) and subcutaneous Keytruda formulation in H2 2026 — both would dramatically expand TAM and extend the earnings duration of the Keytruda franchise
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 12-13x forward earnings — a 35-40% discount to the S&P 500 multiple for a business with 10%+ EPS growth potential through pipeline expansion. The stock has been in the portfolio as a medium-conviction position and the thesis has not been invalidated; the pipeline catalyst timeline is simply longer.
picks.exitTrigger: MK-0616 Phase 3 failure; IRA Medicare drug price negotiation expands aggressively to cut Keytruda reimbursement by more than 25%; Gardasil China volume declines exceed 40% in FY2026
picks.keyRisk: Keytruda IRA negotiation outcome in 2028 is pricing overhang — any acceleration of Medicare negotiation timelines by Congress or CMS would compress the terminal value of Merck's largest revenue asset

picks.name: SPDR Gold Trust
picks.rationale: Gold at ~$4,570 is the cleanest macro hedge available: it performs in stagflation (current regime), in fiscal crisis (rising US debt/GDP), and in risk-off selloffs. Goldman Sachs expects continued gold strength driven by central bank reallocation; real rates are only modestly restrictive at +40bps, the DXY has softened near 102-104, and the Iran conflict provides ongoing safe-haven demand. Zero correlation to S&P 500 in risk-off environments — the ideal portfolio hedge for our late-cycle stagflationary regime call.
picks.catalyst: Any failure of the June 17-18 FOMC (Warsh) to signal rate hikes would cause gold to re-accelerate; Iran escalation or Hormuz closure would trigger immediate safe-haven inflows
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing — June FOMC is the next inflection point
picks.entryNote: Gold has already had a significant run but the structural case for continued appreciation is intact given fiscal deficit expansion, central bank buying, and the Iran geopolitical premium. GLD provides clean exposure without contango or counterparty risk.
picks.exitTrigger: Fed hikes rates to 4.25%+ driving real rates above 100bps — this historically compresses gold as the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding assets rises sharply
picks.keyRisk: Aggressive Fed tightening cycle pushing real rates materially higher — gold's primary structural enemy

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD provides quality-factor + income exposure (yield ~3.5%) with a methodology selecting on FCF/debt, ROE, and 10-year dividend growth — holdings include KO, CVX, VZ, AbbVie, Home Depot. Beta ~0.7, historically 20-25% less severe max drawdown than S&P 500. In a breadth deterioration scenario where mega-cap tech rolls over from extended multiples, SCHD is the natural rotation destination. IG credit spreads near 85-90bps support the underlying dividend payers' balance sheets.
picks.catalyst: Any rotation from growth to value/income — the 2s10s steepening to +55bps historically correlates with outperformance of dividend quality factors over high-multiple growth
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing — accelerates if June FOMC signals hold/hike
picks.entryNote: Near all-time highs but structurally reasonable — the yield spread vs. the 10-year treasury is tighter than historical, but the quality overlay and portfolio hedge function justify the position at any macro entry point.
picks.exitTrigger: Sustained economic acceleration above 3% GDP with no inflation, driving investors back into pure growth at the expense of dividend quality — a Goldilocks scenario that would make defensives unnecessary
picks.keyRisk: The primary holdings (KO, VZ, CVX) are not immune to an energy price collapse or consumer spending drought — broad recession without rate cuts could compress dividend payers alongside growth names

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: Berkshire holds $397B in cash/T-bills earning ~5% risk-free, owns GEICO, BNSF, and the $263B equity portfolio under Greg Abel — the ultimate all-weather compounder. Beta ~0.7, no leverage, and the cash pile alone earns ~$20B/year in T-bill income. Abel's Q1 GOOGL triple-down and restart of buybacks signal disciplined capital deployment. In a risk-off tape from a Fed hike shock, BRK.B is a fortress that benefits from the higher-for-longer rate environment on its T-bill portfolio.
picks.catalyst: Berkshire's Q2 earnings (August) — any major capital deployment from the $397B cash pile into a distressed asset would be a significant positive catalyst; ongoing buybacks are accretive at 1.5x book
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 1.5-1.6x book — a modest premium for an unlevered conglomerate with $397B in liquidity. In a market at 32x trailing P/E, BRK.B at 1.5x book is a relative bargain for the quality and downside protection it provides.
picks.exitTrigger: Greg Abel makes a large acquisition at a poor valuation multiple (above 25x EBITDA) that depletes the cash buffer without adequate return prospect; insurance catastrophe losses exceed $20B in a single year
picks.keyRisk: Reinvestment risk — $397B in cash is a drag on returns in a rising market and Abel must deploy it at reasonable valuations; any major acquisition blunder by the new leadership would sharply reprice the stock

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF (7-10yr duration) provides recession insurance at the intermediate tenor — more defensive than TLT in a stagflation environment while still providing meaningful price appreciation if growth rolls over and the Fed pivots. At 4.12-4.67% yield levels across the 2-10yr curve, the carry alone provides a 4%+ return floor, and the duration profile is a direct hedge against the 30-40% probability tail of recession onset within 12 months. Bonds are NOT the primary hedge but sizing at 4% provides meaningful portfolio ballast without the full duration risk of TLT.
picks.catalyst: Any growth shock below 1% SAAR GDP or credit market disruption would drive a flight-to-quality rally in intermediate treasuries; a below-consensus June CPI print would be the first catalyst
picks.entryNote: The 10yr at 4.67% represents a one-year high and a compelling entry for a 1-2yr horizon if the stagflation tail does not materialize. IEF avoids the convexity risk of TLT (30yr at 5.18%) while still providing duration in a demand-shock scenario.
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year yield breaks sustainably above 5.25% (fiscal crisis/Tail Risk #2 scenario) or June CPI prints above 4.5% forcing the market to price in 75bp+ of Fed hikes — both would materially extend duration losses
picks.keyRisk: Stagflation lock-in scenario where the Fed is forced to hike — IEF loses in a hike cycle the same way TLT does, just less severely; the bond/equity positive correlation means IEF may not hedge the equity book as historically expected

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO provides consumer staples exposure with genuine pricing power, a 64-year dividend growth streak (Dividend King), and near-zero sensitivity to the AI capex cycle that dominates the growth book. In a stagflationary environment where nominal revenues grow with inflation (KO's volumes are inelastic, pricing power demonstrated through 2022-2024), KO is a stable earnings compounder. It remains Berkshire's 3rd-largest holding at 11.56%, providing the ultimate institutional validation of the durable moat thesis.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 organic revenue growth print (July) — any pricing volume mix showing sustained organic growth above 4% despite currency headwinds would validate the inflationary pricing power thesis
picks.entryNote: KO trades at approximately 22-24x forward earnings — fair for a business with global brand oligopoly, 30%+ operating margins, and inflation-passthrough capability. Not cheap but not a yield trap; the 3.2% dividend yield is compelling vs. rising rates given the growth overlay.
picks.exitTrigger: Volume decline sustained for two consecutive quarters as GLP-1 weight loss drug adoption drives structural beverage consumption reduction; emerging market currency collapse compresses USD-reported earnings by 15%+
picks.keyRisk: GLP-1 secular demand destruction — if weight-loss drug adoption accelerates to 20%+ of the US adult population, the long-term volume trajectory of sugared beverages faces structural headwinds that the market has not yet fully priced

weeklyReport.summary: The portfolio is positioned for a late-cycle stagflationary regime — the rarest and most dangerous configuration for long-only equity — where re-accelerating core PCE (3.2% YoY), futures markets pricing Fed rate hikes, and a 3m10y spread oscillating around zero since February 2026 demand a high-conviction, diversified book with explicit downside architecture. The 10-name growth sleeve concentrates in AI infrastructure picks-and-shovels (AVGO, NVDA), AI platform monetizers with clean fundamental setups and no extension flags (GOOGL, AMZN), and cycle-diversifying exposures in Financials (GS, SPGI), Defense (RTX), Energy (XOM), Power Infrastructure (VST), and Healthcare value (MRK) — seven distinct sector exposures with the AI supercycle as the unifying secular thread. From the prior week's book, AVGO was elevated to rank 1 (from rank 2) ahead of the critical June 3 earnings event with $22B revenue guidance far above consensus; GOOGL, NVDA, AMZN, GS, RTX, XOM, SPGI, VST, and MRK are all thesis-intact carry-overs; no names were dropped. The dominant risk being underwritten is a June 17-18 FOMC hike signal from Chair Warsh that would violently reprice the entire growth sleeve — the 5-name defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) collectively hedges this scenario through gold safe-haven, dividend quality rotation, fortress balance sheet exposure, intermediate duration carry, and consumer staples inflation passthrough.
weeklyReport.diversificationNote: The growth book spans 6 distinct GICS sectors: Information Technology (AVGO, NVDA — 2 names), Communication Services (GOOGL — 1), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN — 1), Financials (GS, SPGI — 2), Industrials (RTX — 1), Energy (XOM — 1), Utilities (VST — 1), and Health Care (MRK — 1); the two IT names (AVGO, NVDA) share secular AI exposure but occupy different points in the value chain (custom silicon ASIC vs. GPU training infrastructure). The defensive sleeve spans long-duration fixed income (IEF at 7-10yr), gold (GLD), broad dividend/quality equity (SCHD), all-weather conglomerate (BRK.B), and consumer staples (KO) — no exposure is doubled up, and the sleeve collectively maintains near-zero or negative correlation to the growth book in a risk-off scenario.

phaseData.macro:
Here is your full macro briefing for **Friday, May 29, 2026**:

---

## 📊 US EQUITY MACRO REGIME BRIEFING
### Senior Macro Strategy | May 29, 2026

---

### 🔵 FED POLICY


The Fed has kept the funds rate unchanged at **3.50%–3.75%** for a third consecutive meeting.
 
The April decision was far from consensus — it passed on an **8-4 vote**, the most dissents since October 1992, with Governor Miran voting for a 25bp cut and three others objecting to easing-bias language in the statement.



The March 2026 dot plot (the most recent SEP) sees the funds rate falling just **25bps in 2026 and 25bps in 2027**, with PCE inflation projected to end 2026 at **2.7%** — 30bps higher than the December projection.
 
Of 19 participants, **14 projected either no cut or only one cut** for 2026.



Markets have moved significantly more hawkish than the dot plot: futures pricing has **priced out near-term cuts entirely and is now pricing in rate hikes**.
 
This shift followed reports suggesting inflationary pressures are re-accelerating as rising oil prices tied to the Iran conflict push costs higher — causing traders to bet the next Fed move could be a **hike**, not a cut.


**Real rate context:** With the funds rate at ~3.625% (mid) and core PCE at ~3.2% YoY (see below), the real policy rate is roughly **+40bps** — marginally restrictive but well below the ~150bps of real tightness seen at the 2023 peak. The neutral rate (r*) is widely estimated at 2.5–3.0% nominal, meaning current policy is only modestly above neutral.

---

### 🔵 YIELD CURVE


**2yr: 4.12% | 10yr: 4.67% | 30yr: 5.18%** — all at **2026 year-to-date highs**.


- **2s10s spread: ~+55bps** (positively sloped, re-steepened from deeply inverted 2022–2024 levels)
- 
The 2s10s spread was continuously negative from July 2022 to August 2024, with the last negative reading on **September 5, 2024**.
 
The 3m10y spread was negative from October 2022 through December 2024, and **since February 2026 has oscillated back and forth across zero** — a historically late-cycle warning sign.



The curve has **flattened recently** as short rates have risen more than long rates, while much of the move higher in long yields is attributed to rising **real rates rather than breakeven inflation expectations**.
 Term premium is expanding at the long end as fiscal supply fears and the Iran oil shock drive duration concession.

---

### 🔵 INFLATION


**Core PCE has trended upward** to **3.2% YoY** as of March 2026.
 
Headline inflation is running at approximately **2.8%–3.2% YoY in early 2026** — still above the Fed's 2% target.
 
Inflation is picking up due to the energy shock from the Middle East conflict, even as labor market and broader economic indicators remain resilient.


**Trajectory: RE-ACCELERATING.** Core PCE is moving away from target, not toward it. Supercore (services ex-housing) remains the primary stickiness driver alongside energy passthrough. The disinflation narrative that defined 2024 is broken.

---

### 🔵 GROWTH


The March SEP raised 2026 real GDP growth projections by 10bps to **2.4%**
, though this figure was influenced by government shutdown timing effects. 
Labor market and broader economic indicators remain resilient
, even as the Fed navigates an increasingly complex supply-side inflation environment.

**GDPNow/Atlanta Fed:** No precise current print captured in search; consensus tracking is approximately **+1.5 to 2.0% SAAR** for Q2 2026, reflecting some deceleration from Q1 but not contraction. ISM Manufacturing has been oscillating near the 49–51 contractionary threshold; ISM Services has held above 50 but at lower levels than 2024.

---

### 🔵 CREDIT & LIQUIDITY


Corporate credit spreads have **tightened back near multi-year lows** following the March pullback, with equities rallying to new all-time highs in May.
 HY OAS is approximately **280–300bps** (near post-GFC tights), and IG OAS is near **85–90bps** — both signaling that credit markets are NOT pricing meaningful recession risk. Financial conditions (Goldman FCI, Chicago Fed NFCI) remain accommodative despite the rate backup. The **DXY** has softened modestly from early-year highs on rate-hike uncertainty, currently near **102–104**, reflecting a U.S. exceptionalism trade that is incrementally fading.

---

### 🔵 EARNINGS BACKDROP


The trailing S&P 500 P/E ratio stands at **32.58x**.
 
The S&P 500 Information Technology sector trades at a forward P/E of **28.27x** as of May 29, 2026.
 
Against the 10-year historical median of 24.45x, the current reading ranks in the **76.7th percentile** — historically associated with "overvalued" pricing.
 The broad S&P 500 forward P/E is approximately **21–22x** (vs. the 10-year average of ~18x), leaving little margin for earnings estimate cuts. Forward EPS estimates for the S&P 500 for full-year 2026 cluster around **$270–$280**, implying ~10% growth — estimates that have not yet been revised down to reflect the oil/Iran shock or the potential consumer drag from higher rates.

---

### 🔵 GEOPOLITICAL / EVENT TAPE


The **Iran conflict has driven oil prices up over 50% since hostilities began**, feeding directly into headline CPI and freight/logistics costs.
 
The Fed has explicitly noted that **Middle East developments** are contributing to a high level of uncertainty about the economic outlook.
 
Meanwhile, **Kevin Warsh** assumed the Fed Chair seat on May 15, replacing Powell (who remains as Governor), introducing a potential shift in communication style and policy philosophy.
 The June FOMC meeting (June 17–18) will be Warsh's first as Chair — the most watched event on the calendar.

---

## 🧭 THREE-PARAGRAPH BRIEFING

---

**1. REGIME CALL: Late-Cycle / Stagflationary Transition**

This is a **late-cycle regime with stagflationary characteristics** — the rarest and most dangerous configuration for a long-only equity portfolio. 
Inflation is re-accelerating (core PCE at 3.2% and rising), real rates are moving higher, and futures markets have flipped to pricing Fed *hikes* rather than cuts
, even as 
growth is resilient but decelerating under the weight of energy shock passthrough.
 
The 3m10y spread has oscillated around zero since February 2026
 — historically a final-stage warning that preceded prior recessions within 3–13 months. Credit is behaving like mid-cycle, equities are priced for perfection, but the macro tape is deteriorating. The regime is best characterized as **late-cycle with a 30–40% probability tail of contraction onset within 12 months**.

---

**2. WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING**

**Working:** Energy (direct oil price beneficiary — XLE, XOP), Defense & Aerospace (geopolitical spend, Iran), Commodities broadly, short-duration value names (Financials/Banks, which benefit from a steeper 2s10s and higher-for-longer), and real asset plays (infrastructure, MLPs). 
Risk assets generally have performed strongly into May, with equities at new all-time highs and credit spreads near multi-year tights
 — but this divergence from the macro backdrop is the central risk. **Breaking:** Rate-sensitive sectors (Utilities, REITs) are under structural pressure with the 30yr at 5.18%. Growth/duration equities (Consumer Discretionary, high-multiple Tech) are vulnerable to multiple compression if forward EPS gets cut. 
Tech's forward P/E at the 76.7th percentile of its 10-year range
 means there is minimal cushion. Bonds are NOT the hedge they were in prior cycles — the stock/bond correlation is positive (both selling off together), breaking the traditional 60/40 buffer.

---

**3. WHAT TO WATCH — TOP 2–3 CATALYSTS**

① **June 17–18 FOMC (Warsh's First Meeting as Chair):** 
Investors are closely watching whether a rate hike could still be considered
, and the new SEP/dot plot will reset the entire market's rate path expectations. Any shift toward hike-signaling language would be a significant de-risking event for equities. This is the single highest-impact event in the next 6 weeks.

② **May CPI Print (~June 11) and Core PCE (~June 26):** With 
oil up 50%+ since the Iran conflict began driving re-acceleration in core PCE
, any reading above 3.3–3.5% would materially raise the probability of a hike at the September or November meeting, repricing the entire risk curve. A downside surprise would be the biggest relief catalyst available.

③ **Iran Conflict Escalation / Oil Price:** 
Rising oil prices tied to the conflict with Iran are the primary driver of inflationary re-acceleration fears
. Any de-escalation (ceasefire, diplomatic talks) would rapidly reverse oil, ease CPI trajectory, and restore cut expectations — triggering a significant risk-on rally. Conversely, further escalation (Strait of Hormuz disruption, new theater opening) would be a stagflationary shock of first order, particularly damaging Consumer and Transport names.

---

*Disclosure: This briefing is for informational purposes only and does not constitute investment advice. All data as of May 29, 2026.*

## ⚠️ SECTION 1: SINGLE-STOCK BLOW-UP RISK
### Five Names With Highest Probability of –25%+ Drawdown Over Next 6–12 Months

---

### 🔴 1. TSLA — Tesla (~$386)
**Verdict: Priced for a robotaxi future that is barely off the ground**


Tesla launched a limited unsupervised robotaxi service in Austin in 2025 and expanded to Dallas and Houston in early 2026, but as of April 2026 deployment counts remain modest — 13 unsupervised robotaxis in Austin and two each in Dallas and Houston. Musk has reset the 2026 ambition to operating in "a dozen or so" states.
 The stock prices in a global robotaxi network; current facts price in a pilot.


A jump from ~17 vehicles today to 200+ across multiple states by year-end would re-rate TSLA meaningfully, but a continued slow grind — say, 30–50 vehicles by December — would force the market to mark down the robotaxi optionality and likely compress shares back toward $300.



If Chinese OEMs continue to pressure pricing globally and Tesla responds with further cuts, automotive gross margin could compress again. The cushion from FSD revenue is not yet large enough to fully offset hardware margin erosion.



Morningstar expects deliveries of Tesla's electric vehicles to remain weak in 2026, weighed down by the expiration of the US EV tax credit, rising competition in Europe, and relatively high vehicle prices.



Tesla's $25 billion 2026 capex commitment has telegraphed negative free cash flow for the balance of the year. That posture is defensible while the balance sheet is strong (~$30B in cash), but a second consecutive year of cash burn would re-open the equity-issuance conversation and likely compress the stock.


**Blow-up trigger:** FSD/robotaxi delay, a high-profile safety incident, or China share loss. Downside target: $280–$310 on bear case.

---

### 🔴 2. PLTR — Palantir (~$120+)
**Verdict: Catastrophically over-valued on any fundamental metric**


Investors wanting AI exposure without "concentrating their entire position at 80x trailing sales" are already rotating. A portfolio holding PLTR alongside ServiceNow, Snowflake, or Broadcom captures the AI growth theme while diversifying across valuation profiles.



Palantir has been the most controversial stock this year, with the stock surging during the U.S.-Iran conflict as a critical AI and defense software provider for the U.S. government.
 That geopolitical premium is ephemeral — conflict resolution or defense budget cuts would immediately reprice it. Revenue is heavily government-concentrated; any shift in US defense spending priorities is an existential event. At ~80x sales, the margin of safety is zero.

**Blow-up trigger:** Government contract slowdown, defense budget sequester, or any quarter where commercial growth decelerates below 40% YoY. A simple multiple re-rating from 80x to 40x sales = –50%.

---

### 🔴 3. ARM — Arm Holdings (~$209)
**Verdict: Antitrust overhang + priced-for-perfection multiples**


The U.S. Federal Trade Commission has opened a formal antitrust investigation into Arm Holdings' licensing practices for its semiconductor technology. The probe focuses on how Arm structures and prices access to its chip designs for customers across the global electronics industry.



The investigation introduces fresh regulatory risk around Arm's core licensing model at a time when scrutiny of large technology suppliers is increasing worldwide. This comes after a sharp share price move, with the stock up 31.3% over the past month and 82.3% year-to-date, closing at $209.16. The company has also gained 53.8% over the past year, meaning any regulatory shift affecting licensing could matter more given the current market expectations embedded in the price.



As Yahoo Finance's headline bluntly noted: "ARM Stock Is Valued for Eternity, But Silicon Has an Expiration Date."
 ARM earns royalties on chip volume. If hyperscalers (NVDA, AMD, custom silicon) cut into licensed ARM core adoption, the royalty stream plateaus. The FTC probe threatening the licensing model directly attacks the company's only revenue engine.

**Blow-up trigger:** Adverse FTC ruling forcing licensing price caps or structural separation. Valuation decompression from ~100x forward earnings back to 50–60x = –40%+ drawdown.

---

### 🔴 4. UNH — UnitedHealth Group (~$270)
**Verdict: DOJ antitrust escalation + catastrophic stock collapse already in progress**


The US Department of Justice has intensified an antitrust investigation into UnitedHealth Group's Optum subsidiary and its insurance division. The probe focuses on how the relationship between Optum and the insurance arm may affect competition across healthcare services and insurance markets, introducing additional headline and regulatory risk beyond typical earnings or valuation debates.



The shares recently closed at $269.54, with returns showing declines of 5.6% over the past week, 7.1% over the past month, and 19.9% year to date. Longer term, the stock has seen declines of 46.3% over 1 year and 40.7% over 3 years.



The investigation centers on UnitedHealth's vertically integrated model that links Optum's health services with its insurance operations. Investors may focus less on short-term trading moves and more on how potential remedies, structural changes, or ongoing oversight could affect the company's flexibility to run its combined businesses.


Additionally, per recent reports, UNH secretly paid nursing homes to reduce hospital transfers — a reputational and regulatory double-hit. The integrated model (the core bull thesis) is precisely what regulators are attacking. A forced structural breakup could destroy 30–40% of the embedded multiple.

**Blow-up trigger:** DOJ consent decree requiring Optum divestiture. No floor on the stock until litigation resolution.

---

### 🔴 5. SNOW — Snowflake (~$170–190 range)
**Verdict: Growth re-acceleration priced in but not yet proven; competitive moat narrowing**


Snowflake has quietly grown its cloud and AI business, but its stock has put investors' patience to the test this year despite strong financials.
 The core problem: SNOW trades at ~15–18x forward revenue for a company whose growth rate has decelerated from 70%+ to the mid-20s, facing intensifying competition from Databricks (private, growing faster), Google BigQuery, and Microsoft Fabric directly attacking Snowflake's data warehousing moat. Gross margin compression as compute-heavy AI workloads cannibalize higher-margin storage revenue is the specific financial risk that consensus underestimates.

**Blow-up trigger:** Any Q2/Q3 2026 quarter where NRR (Net Revenue Retention) falls below 125% or RPO growth decelerates below 20%. Downside to $120–130 on a de-rating.

---

### Five Names Combining Durable Moat + Reasonable Valuation + Low-Correlation Behavior

**The cleanest macro hedge in the universe**

Upside exposure in gold and downside in the USD may help to protect against institutional risks resurfacing.



Gold is currently trading near $4,569.90
 — up dramatically YTD. GLD gives direct exposure without contango drag (unlike futures) or counterparty risk. **The definition of an all-weather compounder**

Berkshire holds ~$300B+ in cash/T-bills (earning ~5% risk-free), owns GEICO, BNSF, Berkshire Energy, Apple equity, and a portfolio of value-priced industrials. Global risk assets currently embrace a Goldilocks mindset toward OBBBA tax cuts, European rearmament, and AI capex
 — BRK.B profits from none of this froth and thus doesn't sell off when it unwinds.

### 🟢 3. TLT — iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF
**Duration as recession insurance — with a specific trigger condition**


Bonds are likely to be an effective hedge against the largest risks to equities — a recession or a reversal of AI optimism — particularly at shorter maturities.


The caveat on TLT specifically: 
the 10Y hit 4.601% (a one-year high), with the curve steepening (10Y +24 bps vs 2Y +18.8 bps), pricing durable structural inflation.
 TLT is NOT a "hold-in-all-environments" trade right now — it is specifically a recession/demand-destruction hedge. If growth rolls over and the Fed cuts, TLT outperforms. **Best-in-class industrial gas, recession-resistant, pricing power moat**

Linde is the world's largest industrial gas company with 40%+ EBITDA margins, a monopoly/duopoly position in most markets, and contractual pass-through pricing. ### 🟢 5. SCHD — Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
**Quality factor + income + low volatility**

 In that environment, dividend equities with strong free cash flow (the SCHD methodology selects on FCF/debt, ROE, dividend growth) outperform in slow-growth/volatile regimes. ### Three Highest-Impact Regime-Breaking Scenarios

**Probability: ELEVATED**

**What's already happening:** 
CPI printed 3.8% (Tuesday), PPI 6.0% (Wednesday), import prices +1.9% MoM — double consensus — and rate-hike odds raced from under 3% to over 50% in 72 hours. The 10Y yield surged +24.2 bps to 4.601% (one-year high), and the entire Wall Street bank consensus shifted to zero 2026 cuts.



The cross-asset signature — equities, bonds, gold, and copper all falling while oil, the dollar, and yields rose — is a stagflation pricing event, not a growth scare: markets are assigning durable inflation risk to an unresolved tariff stalemate and an energy shock that will embed in CPI through at least June.


**Trigger:** One more hot CPI print above 4% + Hormuz disruption extending WTI above $100 → Fed signals rate hike → credit spreads violently reprice.

**What gets hurt most:** Long-duration growth stocks (PLTR, SNOW, ARM, NVDA), TLT, REITs (AMT, EQIX, O, PLD), utilities (NEE, D, DUK). XOM, CVX, COP, GLD, and USD *outperform*.

---

### 💥 TAIL RISK #2: US FISCAL CRISIS / BOND VIGILANTE MOMENT
**Probability: MEDIUM (rising)**


The rapid growth of public-sector debt in relation to economic output — particularly in the United States — is an unresolved challenge for global markets. US debt appears to be on a path to rise indefinitely as a share of GDP, soon crossing the 100% threshold. Investors may eventually demand more compensation for bearing this risk.



Global risk assets currently embrace a Goldilocks mindset toward One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) tax cuts
 — which structurally widens the deficit by ~$3–4T over 10 years. If Treasury issuance overwhelms demand, term premium spikes.

**Trigger:** Failed Treasury auction, Moody's/Fitch downgrade, or 10Y yield breaking 5.25%+ with no growth catalyst.

**What gets hurt most:** Everything with a "long duration" characteristic — growth tech, REITs, utilities, TLT. Paradoxically, short-term T-bills (IEF) and gold hold. USD could rally initially then weaken on credibility loss.

### 💥 TAIL RISK #3: AI CAPEX DISAPPOINTMENT / HYPERSCALER SPEND REVERSAL
**Probability: MEDIUM**

The entire 2024–2026 bull market in semiconductors and AI software rests on one thesis: that hyperscaler (MSFT, GOOGL, AMZN, META) capex of $300B+ per year is durable.

, against a 10-year Treasury at 4.30% at the time — meaning equities offer a **negative equity risk premium** vs. bonds.

 in early May, showing the binary nature of this risk.

---

## 📝 THE THREE PARAGRAPHS

---

### ¶1 — **REGIME CALL: Late-Cycle / Stagflationary Transition**

This is a **late-cycle regime with stagflationary overtones** — arguably the most difficult backdrop for long-only equity management. 
The FOMC has been on hold for five consecutive meetings
, with real rates meaningfully positive: the funds rate at 3.50–3.75% minus ~3.0% core inflation implies a real policy rate of roughly **+50 to +75 bps** — modestly above neutral but not aggressively so, keeping financial conditions tighter than the cycle warrants. 
The ISM Services Prices index at 70.7 — the highest sustained input-cost gauge in over four years
 — combined with 
a 4.3% unemployment rate
 is the textbook late-cycle signal: labor markets still firm, but inflation refusing to clear. 
HY spreads at the 14th percentile and IG at the 26th
 signal credit markets are still complacent, which historically precedes spread widening in late-cycle regimes. The Iran conflict has introduced a **negative supply shock** that compresses the Fed's easing optionality precisely when growth risks are building.

---

### ¶2 — **What's Working / What's Breaking**

**Working:** Energy names are the clearest late-cycle/conflict beneficiary — 
energy sector EPS estimates have just started to get a boost
 from elevated oil prices. Short-duration fixed income (T-bills, 0–2yr Treasuries) continues to benefit from a high nominal carry environment. Quality-factor within equities (high ROIC, low leverage) is outperforming. 
Technology sector EPS estimates have been revised sharply higher since mid-February 2026
, driven by AI capex (Oracle, Micron), keeping mega-cap tech resilient even as the broader index lags. Defense/industrials tied to geopolitical rearmament remain bid. **Breaking:** Rate-sensitive sectors — REITs, utilities, homebuilders — face dual headwinds from a sticky 10-year at 4.45% and 
30-year mortgage rates at 6.53%
. 
The asymmetry continues to favor a modest underweight to long-duration risk, with a quality bias in credit — a tilt toward shorter-dated IG and away from CCC-rated high yield.
 Small-caps (most rate-sensitive, most Iran-exposed via domestic cost pass-through) are underperforming. Consumer discretionary is squeezed by sticky inflation eroding real purchasing power.

---

### ¶3 — **What to Watch — Top 2–3 Catalysts (1–6 Month Horizon)**

1. **June 16–17 FOMC + June Dot Plot (SEP):** 
The Summary of Economic Projections ("dot plot") is released quarterly: March, June, September, and December.
 The June SEP is the highest-stakes update of 2026. Any hawkish revision — a skip in the median cut path, or an explicit acknowledgment that the next move could be a hike — would reprice the front end violently and compress equity multiples. 
Iran-driven oil inflation has already caused some traders to bet the next Fed move is a hike rather than a cut
, making this meeting binary for risk assets.

2. **May CPI (release ~June 11) & Core PCE:** With 
inflation running 2.8%–3.2% YoY
 and ISM Services Prices at a four-year high, a hot May CPI print (>0.4% MoM core) would be the single most damaging near-term catalyst — simultaneously killing rate-cut hopes, pushing the 10-year through 4.60–4.70%, and triggering multiple compression across all equity sectors.

3. **Iran / Strait of Hormuz Escalation or Resolution:** 
Crude oil declined more than 6% on Strait of Hormuz de-escalation hopes
, demonstrating the outsized market impact of this geopolitical variable. A genuine diplomatic resolution would be the biggest positive macro surprise of 2026 — dropping oil 10–15%, easing inflation expectations, reopening the Fed's easing path, and triggering a sharp risk-on rotation into cyclicals and small-caps. Conversely, further escalation (Hormuz closure, Iranian mining) would be a stagflationary shock of the first order. This is the asymmetric tail that dominates the 1–6 month risk horizon.

### ⚡ TAIL RISK #1: U.S. Fiscal Crisis / Bond Vigilante Shock
**Probability: ELEVATED**

**What has already happened:** 
On May 16, 2025, Moody's downgraded the United States from Aaa to Aa1 — the first time in history that no major credit rating agency holds the U.S. at a pristine AAA rating.
 
By late May 2026, the 30-year U.S. Treasury yield was sitting at 5.08%.


**The structural math:** 
The CBO estimated the "One Big Beautiful Bill" would add $3.4 trillion to the federal deficit over 2025–2034.
 
Moody's projects the U.S. federal deficit will rise from 6.4% of GDP in 2024 to 9% by 2035, while debt-to-GDP jumps from 98% to 134%.


**What would trigger escalation:** A failed Treasury auction, a further sovereign credit rating action, or the debt ceiling X-date (estimated by late summer 2026) without Congressional resolution. 
Treasury Secretary Bessent indicated the government could hit the so-called X-date — when it runs out of cash to meet obligations — by August.


**What gets hurt most:** Long-duration assets (TLT crushed), high-multiple growth equities (PLTR, NVDA, TSLA re-rate violently), REITs (AMT, EQIX, PLD), utilities. 
A steeper term premium would likely drag on equity valuations, widen corporate credit spreads, increase mortgage rates, and spur greater capital outflows from U.S. assets — exerting additional pressure on the U.S. dollar.

### ⚡ TAIL RISK #2: AI Capex Cycle Reversal / "ROI Recession"
**Probability: MEDIUM**

**The setup:** 
The AI boom is a source of potential market risk. AI has been a much larger story for markets than for the economy so far.
 The question markets haven't yet priced: what happens if Microsoft, Google, Meta, and Amazon collectively signal that $300B+ in annual AI capex is producing insufficient commercial returns? 
The key risk is that the equity market questions the value of AI-related revenues.


**What would trigger it:** Two consecutive quarters of hyperscaler capex guidance cuts; a high-profile AI product failure (e.g., an autonomous system liability event); regulatory restrictions on AI deployment in Europe or the U.S.; or a major cloud customer loudly abandoning AI-driven SaaS subscriptions. A single Nvidia revenue guide-down citing demand normalization would cascade.

**What gets hurt most:** NVDA (–40%+ scenario), AMD, MRVL, ANET, ARM, SNOW, PLTR, CRM, NOW. The entire AI infrastructure stack — which has consumed the lion's share of index weight gains since 2023 — reprices simultaneously. This is the 2000 "fiber overbuild" analogue.
